architecture.summary: The paper studies sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that decompose neural network activations into interpretable features. SAEs map high-dimensional activations through a dictionary matrix and ReLU nonlinearity to produce sparse feature vectors. The core mechanism examined is how dimension-level activation outliers—dimensions with large mean magnitude relative to per-token variation—shift pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean, causing features anti-aligned with the mean to receive permanently negative pre-activations and never activate (feature death). The paper formalizes outlier severity as γ = ‖μ‖/‖σ‖ and proposes mean-centering as a solution.

biblio.title: On the Relationship Between Activation Outliers and Feature Death in Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: Death rates vary dramatically between models: near-zero on GPT-2, over 70% on AlphaFold3 with identical configurations

claims.dataset: 454 model-layer combinations (language, vision, protein, genomic models)
claims.statement: Outlier severity γ = ‖μ‖/‖σ‖ predicts initial death rates (Spearman ρ = 0.89 for dead-by-TopK, 0.82 for dead-by-ReLU) across 454 model-layer combinations
Spearman ρ = 0.82 for dead-by-ReLU across 454 model-layer combinations
Features anti-aligned with the activation mean receive permanently negative pre-activations and never fire
Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires the SAE bias to learn the activation mean, a process that is prohibitively slow at high γ
Mean-centering (subtracting the activation mean) sidesteps outlier-induced death and eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models
Dimension-level activation outliers cause feature death by shifting pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean
